item.title: Why is this so hard to understand?
item.description: https://apple.news/ASOTal6sGTCyzM2kuHDHhPQ Dear Democrat Party, The people don’t want moderates. The people don’t want status quo. The people don’t want a return to “normal”. The people want you to admit the system is broken and fix it! We have a dying, decrepit system in the US that we’re tired of. Greedflation, stagnant min wage, horrible medical insurance, housing shortages…all of it. Trump didn’t start any of that, he’s just a robber baron swooping in to take advantage of the corpse lying on the side of the road. Hell, the reason Trump won over so many new voters this time was a promise of making our lives better. That people actually believed him is on them, but change is the populist platform. We (of the non MAGA crowd) want change too, we just want those things done constructively to benefit the people and not destructively to benefit Trump and his cronies. In short, dear Democrat Party, please stop trying to be the sane parent that’s holding the marriage together and be the parent who will do whatever it takes to protect the kids from the crazy, abusive parent.
item.dc:creator: Kammel

item.title: Going, going, Gone.
item.description: A Cybertr, erm, WankPanzer has sunk in the harbor in Ventura, Calif. after its driver attempted to load a jet ski into the water. Musk previously claimed the WankPanzer could be used as a boat for a short period of time, allowing the vehicle to cross rivers.

item.dc:creator: DerpeDimmer

item.title: Facebook marketing scam breakdown
item.description:
this post was filled with comments of people saying they loved "Paul Richard" bags and AI images of them holding them up.
Paul is AI
in december of 2024, this website didn't exist
The current website looks like a legitimate retail website.
the AI portrait of "Paul Richard" is superficially similar but not even close to looking like the same person when viewed up close.
even at 70% off, these are 200+ dollar bags.
this is not the first Paul Richard website though, this scam has been ran multiple times. https://www.paul-richard.com/ was the previous URL and it is now dead. People pay money, never get an item and if they try to follow up, the website is gone and there's no way to contact the seller. Stay safe out there!

item.title: What Happened Today #39
item.description: ? The scale of today’s upheaval is staggering: Ukraine strikes a fragile ceasefire as U.S. aid resumes, but back home, education, food assistance, and climate protections are all on the chopping block. NOAA faces another round of firings, the EPA is gutting its environmental justice offices, and whistleblowers are sounding the alarm on plans to dismantle the CFPB. Meanwhile, Musk is pouring millions into Trump’s political machine, and the White House insists slashing Social Security funding won’t touch benefits. ? The consequences of these decisions will outlast the headlines. Stay informed, speak out, and demand accountability. Get involved in your community and with your elected officials—because staying on the sidelines isn’t an option. #Resist #GovBriefToday 1. https://www.bbc.com/news/live/c51ypekv9xwt 2. https://www.nbcnews.com/politics/congress/house-vote-funding-bill-avert-government-shutdown-rcna195808 3. https://federalnewsnetwork.com/workforce/2025/03/education-dept-announces-temporary-office-closures-citing-security-reasons/ 4. https://www.cbsnews.com/news/usaid-staff-instructed-to-shred-or-burn-classified-safe-documents/ 5. https://apnews.com/article/noaa-job-cuts-weather-forecasts-trump-doge-musk-7e35e9d5d757d8fc3f0f50b2bd71c87d 6. https://www.cnn.com/2025/03/11/politics/musk-federal-entitlements-costs/index.html 7. https://www.spokesman.com/stories/2025/mar/11/trump-administration-slashes-division-in-charge-of/ 8. https://www.washingtonpost.com/dc-md-va/2025/03/11/martin-vindman-ukraine-us-attorney/ 9. https://www.usnews.com/news/world/articles/2025-03-11/trump-overstepped-his-constitutional-authority-in-freezing-congress-funding-for-usaid-judge-says 10. https://www.msn.com/en-us/news/us/trump-administration-says-it-could-take-months-to-resume-refugee-admissions/ar-AA1AHCaW 11. https://www.nytimes.com/2025/03/11/climate/epa-closure-environmental-justice-offices.html 12. https://www.axios.com/2025/03/12/musk-trump-100-million-donation-political-operation 13. https://www.cnn.com/2025/03/11/business/tesla-stock-trump-white-house/index.html 14. https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2025/mar/11/trump-administration-teacher-training-blocked 15. https://abcnews.go.com/Politics/rep-sarah-mcbride-misgendered-republican-colleague-committee-hearing/story?id=119692546 16. https://www.cbsnews.com/news/usda-cancels-local-food-purchasing-food-banks-school-meals/ FIGHTING BACK: https://thehill.com/regulation/court-battles/5189503-cfpb-employees-testify-layoffs/
item.dc:creator: GeorgeBounacos

item.title: I made a video of a teriyaki bottle going through an existential crisis
item.description:
Why would you make this poor teriyaki bottle go through an existential crisis? Good question. Basically I saw a creative contest for a trip to Japan that literally just said “make anything creative using this Teriyaki bottle”. Super vague requirements so naturally I made a video about the teriyaki bottle going through an existential crisis and he just wants to go back home to Japan to see his family.
Thought you guys might think it’s funny. Let me know what you think. Does it make sense? I was trying to go for the “early 2000s Chef Boyardee can comes to life and chases after little girl” vibe.
I made him a little hobo bindle with a chopstick, as one does when running away.
I made it into the top 6 so fingers crossed. Hopefully he gets his happy ending in Japan…wait no, I mean with his family. FP Edit: Appreciate you guys! I’m glad the story comes through. If anyone wants to help vote for my video I’d really appreciate it but no pressure: www.kikkomanteriyakithatchallenge.com/vote
item.dc:creator: ItsMoni

item.title: Needless to say, the family dinner was awkward.
item.description: My siblings (much older than me) are very lucky that they have been able to stay in the same companies and move consistently upwards. That is not normal these days. At least in Europe you get things like benefits, public transportation (usually) and vacation days. I was astonished that my current job gave me 96 hours (12 days) of PTO a year as it was almost double my previous job and the other offer I received. They responded about needing to pay US & European taxes. “Well that’s only if I remain a US citizen, which I am seeing less and less reason to.”

item.title: Choppy - stand back and stand by!
item.dc:creator: LollipopsCorner

item.title: Disassembly and Polishing of the Ugly Duckling
item.description:
Hey y'all, remember this thing? I hope so, I posted about it yesterday! https://imgur.com/gallery/saddle-up-new-restoration-pS6tcjS Imgur's API was being estupido last night and kicking back all the little video segments I recorded, so it was less a starter post and more of an introduction to the machine. So, now that we're acquainted, it's time for the chunky-ass post where you get way too much granular information about the disassembly process! Woo! Those of you who just want to look at the pretty pictures and scroll are forgiven. I'm probably going to get rambly in this one. ;) (Want to help with the restoration? :) https://ko-fi.com/elbowdeepinahorse/)
So! Quick refresher, this is a National Cash Register that was originally sold to an unknown business out of a Wausau, WI store in 1913. At over 110 years old, it has a lot of visible wear and tear that goes a beyond what I normally see on these old things. This cash register, sadly, was well loved and then completely forgotten somewhere it was allowed to gather dust, grime, moisture, and mice. I'm frankly surprised it even turns over, let alone displays the correct information behind the windows. These things were built tough before Ford stole the motto. Anyhoo, the keen-eared among you probably noticed something missing when I cranked this over. No bell. There's an audible clank, but no ring. I haven't investigated the cause just yet, but experience tells me it's either a cracked bell or the ringer is locked and it's just striking a stop mechanism instead. Something to add to the to-do.
As with every disassembly, the easy stuff comes off first. Namely the decorative cover around the printer mechanism. As I'm taking this register apart, I'm already aware that the surface finish does not look like it's in good condition at all. There's a lot of dullness, discoloration, and visible oxidation. Worst part is, none of it's evenly spread. It's a little of this, and a little of that. Blotchy, not consistent. Not a good sign.
These come off by pressing the silver knob down in the center and just lifting it away. Easy peasy.
Inside is a standard receipt printer with some rust spotting, but nothing that jumps out as a red flag. Mostly, it's just dirty.
This, however, caught my eye. This should be flush, not poking out.
Turns out it's a receipt stamp, and one that has been so thoroughly abused that there's barely any raised text to read. Judging by how clean it is, I think the previous owners had taken a crack at cleaning it up and were way too aggressive with it. Probably used steel brushes or an abrasive pad, given how the lettering looks sheared off rather than stamped flat. I'll spend some time with it to see if I can't get a sense of what it says, but I think it's DOA.
It wasn't able to seat into the printer because there's paper jammed inside. Five seconds of work pulled this free. Boggles my mind that the last owners didn't do this already.
In you go, nice and safe.
Thought I'd add a visual of the printer in action while the register cranks over. I'll be the first to admit I don't understand each mechanism just yet, only a few. It's hard to visualize what everything is doing when paper is so hard to find for it.
Anyway, back to disassembly, starting with more of the easy stuff. The crank handle's gotta go!
Then it's straight to the top to remove the shroud around the price indicators. This comes off like a hat. Two screws and up it goes, though I wasn't about to try filming myself lifting this thing up while freehanding my phone. With my luck, I'd drop it.
To help myself stay organized, I take a photo of each part I remove alongside the fasteners that held it on. That way if I get lost on the rebuild, I can backtrack the way I came without using the wrong screws for the wrong steps.
Of course this first part is actually two separate parts, because why not? Hello, rusty screws! The top sandwiches down onto the bottom plate, and the glass windows are held together between them.
And there we go! Two perfectly intact panes of original glass, too! How's that for luck?
I got this little electric screwdriver for Christmas and it's a freaking wrist saver. Great for backing out stuck screws without getting zingers up my hands. I don't think I'll use it to screw everything back down, though, but we'll see. Oh, also, enjoy the close-up of the CRUD. All those indentations in the scrollwork are filled to the top with nothing but barn gunk, and it's anyone's guess if there's any brass left underneath it. I'd be more hopeful if it flaked off, but it comes up like poster buddy which means it's holding moisture. No bueno.
Next come all the little locks and accessories. Where I can see exposed brass, I'm also seeing hairline cracks. I should open a red flag warehouse, I have so many.
This lock cover's not faring any better. Plenty of green crust as well as spots where the brass is worn down to the bronze casting. You can actually see how the dust and crud settled along the bottom of each indentation over decades and was never cleaned away.
The breastplate is able to come off now that all the geegaws are removed. You can see exactly where the covers protected the brass and where it just gathered grime. What an absolute bummer.
Reference photo for myself to tell me where each screw for the breastplate goes, and also to let myself know it was originally missing its bottom right screw so I don't go robbing Peter to pay Paul without realizing it. I probably have a spare lying around somewhere, so it's no bit whoop.
On the back of the breastplate is the last lock, whose purpose I actually don't know yet. I haven't ever had a key for this one. Either way, it's gotta come off.
Easy peasy, just undo that one screw apparently.
Ah. Nope, gotta turn the lock to reveal the *other* screw. Clever girl.
Hopefully this is the bell lock. If so, it might make figuring out why the bell isn't ringing that much easier. But since I took it all apart before remembering to test it... whoops. Gotta wait, now. :D
I nearly forgot to pull off these accent pieces.
And lastly, the little scrollwork cover that rounds out the decorative printer encasement.
With that done, I need to stow the marble shelf. This thing is eight layers of filthy, and you can hear the amount of crud trapped beneath it.
Hello mouse nest my old friend~
Moving on, the front cover of the price display needs to come off.
As well as all those little glass windows on the other side of it. Lots of teeny screws and little purpose built frames to hold the glass in place. Simple and easy.
Same goes for the main window and its framing. Once again, intact glass. No chips. Me happy about that. :)
Now that the front is all taken care of, we swivel around to the back. This screws into each side panel for support, so it needs to come off before they can.
Poof! Magic! Easy enough to see that the back of this hasn't been opened since my grandmother was a teenager because it's damn near pristine.
Unlike the panel itself, which is just... it's had a bad time of things. I don't even know where to start. There's the omnipresent grime, speckles of spilled paint, drips where maybe water condensed or something was dribbled over the back of it. You can even see where the vertical lines acted as easy paths for something to run down to the bottom and somehow keep the brass from tarnishing altogether. I don't even know how that works. Not to mention the low spots have a rough, almost pitted texture to them under the fingernail. Not smooth like it's meant to feel. Sigh...
No sense in kicking pebbles and feeling sorry for myself. Time to get this last window off.
And another intact piece. Man, I'll take it.
That just leaves us with the side panels. As with any register with a printer attached, the printer panel is always sectioned into two pieces that join behind the mechanism.
I kinda like how this one was planned out so that the seams are barely visible even if you look for them. Also, they're filthier than my mind on a night off.
And lastly, the crank side panel. More red flags. More uneven discoloring, blotches, clear slash marks where matches were struck against the brass which was common enough. Where I can see bare brass, once more I can also see cracking. This machine has seen some shit and I have no idea how it'll look once all that crap is cleaned off.
The mandatory flip to get at the gadgets attached to the other side. In this case, the counter mechanism that tracks how many transactions were registered since it was last reset.
Two screws, and the final piece of glass is intact as well. That's six out of six. Helluva record considering how the rest of this poor thing looks.
Something tells me this was used more than 265 times. Someone's been playing with the reset key.
Oh, I nearly forgot to take off the windows for the hidden counters. These keep track of the total amount of dollars and cents taken in as well as the amount of special transactions. Only an owner's key can slide the windows open to record those totals. Not sure if I have one, now that I think of it.
At least this'll clean up easily enough.
Evidence of someone doing a quick spit and smear to see what the serial number was. Clearly didn't care enough to actually clean the rest of the machine, but I'm not judging or anything. <_<
Gotta check for varmints, of which I can't see any.
Also gotta take the NCR plate off before I forget.
And of course, back to the printer box. I need to pull off all the locks and doors.
This just pops out with a good pull. The bracket with the hole in the center aligns with the post that the printer paper sits on, keeping the door from rattling in its frame.
Another good reason to photograph everything I take off. After a while, all these screws start to look the same. Also, that spring is tiny as hell and I don't feel like losing it.
And off come the rest of the locks and levers.
This guy helps keep the cover attached to the register's frame, and I found that this one doesn't screw off. It's riveted in. I pulled off the spring so it wouldn't get damaged during cleaning and left the lever in place.
And finally, all these brass strips and counter covers have to come off.
These screws are tiny and super shallow. So much so that I had to be careful that I was aligning the driver with the slot, and not some bit of hair caught in the muck caked over them. If you look closely, before I start twisting the screw off I put some pressure on the driver. You can see the gunk push out of the slot like the world's nastiest Play-Doh. Absolutely nasty.
Luckly, I managed not to lose any of the screws. The keys and their stems are surprisingly un-cruddy for the most part, but I'm sure I'll find something that I overlooked once I go about checking this whole mechanism over for stickiness.
I was able to lift this thing out of the cash box without tweaking my back this time. Amazing how much weight gets removed when there's one less column of buttons on a machine!
Not too thrilled to see this. I normally don't make a point to clean this part of the register anyway, as the paint is usually fragile and will come off the metal if you work at it too much. But I do want to get that grime off the posts and out of that rotating red bar. Plus, each of the wheels have a deposit where the numbers faced upward. That's going to be the least fun, trying to figure out when too much is too much before I start pulling paint off the numbers.
The frame is nasty. I feel like describing it more than that would be beating a dead horse. (nyuck nyuck nyuck)
The oak cash box isn't much better. It's not quite to the point of dry rotting, but it's on its way there. The wood is to the point where it's nearly desiccated where I can actually see it. Obviously, I'm not going to remove or replace any of it when it's still perfectly capable of serving its original purpose. And the crap that has congealed under the register, just like the last one I restored, is just going to have to be happy with being peeled off and allowed to stain whatever's under it. Oh, also, mold colonies. Yum yum.
Hm. Now what's all this then. I do believe those are dimes!
And that doesn't look like a stamp you'd stick on an envelope.
Well how's that for a ray of sunshine on an otherwise gloomy project? Four silver barber dimes and two silver Mercury dimes, plus a pair of war ration stamps! Hot damn, get me a time machine to 1943 and I could get myself a pound of beef and a sack of groceries! :D Now, the ration stamps aren't worth anything, I checked. You can buy entire books of them for a few bucks on eBay. I'm gonna keep them though because, why not? They're cool! As for the dimes, I looked each of them over and I won't be buying a new car with any of them either. They're old as dirt and in fairly okay condition, but none of them hit that magical combination of "rare year" and "rare mint stamp." All of the barber dimes were minted in Philadelphia, which makes that 1895 coin worth maaaybe five bucks? Ah well, more cool coins to add to that little pocket dispenser I restored last year. :)
So! Now I've got a heap of brass panels and a bunch of loose parts. Nothing to do now besides clean them up and see what the metal looks like under all of that grime.
First, all the fasteners and hinges get chucked into the sonic bath with some degreaser. I kept them in there for about fifteen minutes.
Overall, not too bad. I'll clean a few of them off with a toothbrush and that'll be that. Beats cleaning them all by hand.
As for the brass... yeah. So, about the brass.
For reference, this is that big rear panel with all the staining and uneven discoloration. My basement's lighting isn't great, so it does make all these look a little more dramatic than they are.
And these are the rest of the pieces laid out before I let them soak for a bit in a bath of Dawn soap with degreaser. There's no sense in putting any of these under a buffing wheel with all this junk caked onto the metal, so they need to be washed down to the bare brass first.
I am not exaggerating when I say I spent more than an hour sitting at the utility sink scrubbing every one of these panels again and again with a stiff plastic bristle brush. I know some of you will want to know why I don't use a brass bristle brush, and the reasoning is because I didn't want to use anything that would lift away any loose plating. It's also worth saying that I just don't trust stores when they say a brush has brass bristles. Sometimes they do, and sometimes they're just steel bristles plated with brass. The last thing I want to do is get duped by a steel brush and obliterate an antique without even realizing it. So the lighter touch prevails.
Removing all the grime was a pain in the ass, and what I was left with when it was done wasn't heartening. You can see how all the discoloration is still there, and much more stark. Some of it will clear away under the polishing wheel, because that's just what tarnish does, but my concern is that not all of the splotching is the same color or darkness. That may mean the brass plating has been affected differently by whatever was attacking it.
I spent a fair bit of time with each piece under the buffing wheel, applying jeweler's rouge at different angles and directions to be sure I was hitting all of the low spots as completely as possible. Overall, I think the smooth spots look alright. I can still see places where the brass is brighter or darker where it reacted with whatever had gotten spilled over it, but going at those spots harder with the polisher wasn't doing anything to make it look uniform. So there's something going on with the metal itself that an abrasive isn't fixing. Rather than hammer away at it until I break through to the bronze casting, I'm choosing to leave it be. As for the low spots, I'm still seeing a lot of the splotching I saw during disassembly.
This is more of a glamor shot than any proof that everything was magically fixed by polishing it. The high spots are as close to a mirror finish as I could get them, but the low spots still look pretty uneven and ugly. Honestly, the closer I look at those areas, the more I'm seeing spots where the brass plating has been eaten away to expose the bronze layer. That can't be fixed with polishing. Sadly, whatever was allowed to sit on the outer panels managed to eat away a *lot* of brass.
Thankfully, a few pieces look pretty nice after being polished. The printer cover is pretty snazzy looking, at least along the top. Still, I'm not satisfied with how the rest looks. Before I reassemble everything, I'm going to attempt something cosmetic that I haven't ever tried before. For now I'm going to keep that a secret. Hopefully it works out and I don't make a total ass out of myself. >_<
Anyhoo, with the brass paneling as done as they can reasonably be, it was time to look over the cash box and see what could be done with it. With my last restoration, I was able to wash the wood and oil it back to a healthy shine. That's not the case for this one. It was allowed to dry out to the point that most of the original veneer has flaked away, the wood itself has started to crack along the grain, and it's way beyond the point of splashing on a bottle of Murphy's Wood Soap.
This is what my freaking fingers look like in winter. No bueno.
This is what it looked like after washing it with soap and degreaser. Better, but only because it's shiny and damp. Rather than do a half-assed job trying to cover it up, I decided to let it dry and then break out the sandpaper.
Since the rest of the box looks fine, I'm only sanding the top. Getting through that last layer of grime was stubborn work, but eventually I was starting to see naked wood.
I'm using 100 grit sandpaper, then bumping up to 200 grit for the last pass. I didn't want to get too excessive and take away more than just the surface layer. Where the cash register itself sat on the box, it dug grooves into the grain, probably because the bolts holding them together were tightened too much. Since it won't be visible after reassembly, I'm not worrying about filling in the grooves or sanding them smooth. I'm also not going to try to remove the spots where the wood got stained along the edge. That's probably penetrated way too deep into the grain to be worth chasing, so we'll just say it adds character and stop sanding here.
After sweeping off the dust and wiping it down with a damp cloth, I let the top dry out and then came down to add some stain. I like using Minwax's Aged Oak stain for the wood on these registers because it tends to match the original color more or less to a T. I put on two layers, making sure to let them overlap a little around the outer lip so there's not an obvious change in shades. Once I'm confident the wood's dry, I'll wash all the sides and bottom and go over everything with tung oil to rehydrate the wood. More than likely I'll end up doing this twice, and hopefully by the time I finish doing what I plan to do with the panels, it'll be dry enough to seal with some beeswax.
Obligatory Story tax, because this was hard weekend work and this poor kitty was all tuckered out from watching me toil away. <3
So overall, I have some big things left to do. I have to finish these panels, for one. Finishing the cash box is two. Cleaning out the cash drawer and fixing any issues it may have is three. Not to mention cleaning the crud off all the glass panes, figuring out why the bell isn't ringing, checking the mechanism for any defects, and cleaning up all the keys and stems before final reassembly. So yeah, lots left to do! Here's hoping I don't screw it up! :D
item.dc:creator: ElbowDeepInAHorse

item.dc:creator: Fulustreka

item.title: Doggo and his new wheels!
item.dc:creator: JasonM1

item.title: Not really.
item.dc:creator: countervail247365

item.title: Is this a "green flag"?
item.description: Is this what one should look for in a man? Is this what secure masculinity looks like? I should sleep on this. Edit: I did not expect this to blow up, but I'm glad you lot enjoyed it. Just to be clear, I'm pretty sure the guy in the video was being humorous and wasn't actually expecting it to escalate. He simply processed the situation well and is clearly secure in himself. I woke up realising my last two relationships (the longest) have been with men who have fragile egos. It's going to take time to unlearn a lot of that from this past decade. Or I could figure out how to get involved in the current hellstorm of this political climate and contribute to the betterment of society rather than feed my selfish need for affection. I've also never asked for anything on my MV posts, but let's see if this works... pls send me pics of your pups/kitties + their breeds, and/or names (pics, if you oblige) of your favourite sweets, so that I can try recreating them in a more nutritious form (without white carbs and processed sugars).
item.dc:creator: unwaver

item.title: Return of the Meme: 1298 (spice vibe)
item.description:
"No one is finally dead until the ripples they cause in the world die away, until the clock wound up winds down, until the wine she made has finished its ferment, until the crop they planted is harvested. The span of someone’s life is only the core of their actual existence." --Terry Pratchett, in "Reaper Man" Sir Terry left us 10 years ago today. I miss that man. Let's get this bread.
Yes, because native folklore such as the Mapinguari TOTALLY means there's still ground sloths in the Amazon.
Hell yes.
The Cowbird Mafia is a thing.
Maybe it's just me being outside of the loop, but I'm honestly surprised Spinosaurus isn't a huge Trans icon.
I admit, I used to dislike Constantine V, but I am now thinking he really got a bad rap by chroniclers with an axe to grind. You can't be incompetent and stupid and hold an empire together.
Oh look a civil war.
We should perform this punishment on ol' Musky and The Orange One.
Oh, Calligula.
This also applies to Spotted Towhees. I did finally get some nice photos of Spotted Towhees, though.
Vimes would readily agree he's a bastard.
Mood.
This reminds me, I need to find someone who can operate my camera for a photoshoot.
The fact that nobody has anything controversial to say about him 10 years after he's shed his mortal coil really says something about the dude. Unlike his Good Omens cohort...
The Inca army
I kind of miss the days of writing academic papers, which is absolutely wild. I think it was the thrill of connecting things in my brain.
Was it @Ronelyn who said she uses these exclusively, or am I hallucinating again?
Mood.
I feel the urge to play Gwent again, maybe I should download The Witcher 3: Gwent Simulator again, but there's all that annoying RPG play that goes with the card game that I really don't feel like doing over again.
This. Even if that tyrant is me, I want her to be willing to use that spike. (This is not a joke about pegging. This is a joke about homicide.)
I once broke a bunch of pottery and dishes I made and threw them into a trench in my backyard.
Would you like to know more about the European Early Bronze Age? [Y] [Y]
We can only hope.
It's me. Or Pippin.
Also conures. Speaking of, found an artist on bluesky who is working on the art for the T-shirt, AND someone suggested stickers. What's a good sticker-selling website?
I haven't experienced this in.... ugh. Ugh. Maybe a minimum of 7 years? Ah well. So it goes. Gotta keep going.
Pippin tax! Today's message from Pippin: "whatdoing? Glory Pippin. ______ 1) Are you on the run to a safe place and passing through Arizona on your way to safer grounds? My apartment is open to you to crash at, and it is a safe space. Pippin and I will make you a meal and gladly share tea, wine, or mead with you. 2) Hey! If you're a woman or minority in a STEM field in Arizona living north of Phoenix and you need new headshots for this website or another detailing your experiences/research/accomplishment because our monstrous regime has deleted your experience from an official government website, PM me. Pippin and I have a good Nikon DSLR, and we'll do the photos for free, and, uh, you'll get to meet Pippin, I guess? (If you know someone who could use this who's not on Imgur, feel free to PM me to get my email address for them and tell them they're free to message me.) 3) Take your meds with a full glass of water, please. Do it for Pippin, or Space Mom, or better yet, do it for yourself 4) Ĝefeloað wē. We will carry on.
